title: Lesson 5: The Church
description: God did not save you to walk alone. He saved you into a family. This lesson explores what the church really is, why you belong, and what it looks like to move from spectator to family member.

blocks.text: In the last lesson you learned how to read the Bible using the Three Discovery Questions. You now have a way to hear from God every time you open His Word. But here is something important: God never intended for you to do any of this alone. He did not just save you. He saved you into a family. That family is the church, and it is one of the greatest gifts God gives to every believer.
The Church Is a Plural Word

When the Bible talks about the church, it is not talking about a building with a steeple. It is not talking about a Sunday morning event you attend. The word "church" means a gathered people, called out by God, drawn into a family by the gospel. It is a plural word, so you cannot be the church by yourself.

When you became a follower of Jesus, you were not just starting a personal journey. You were joined to a family that stretches across the whole world and all the way back to the very first believers. God sent the Holy Spirit to empower and unify this family around the gospel. Being planted in a local church is how you experience that Christ-centered community firsthand.

And they devoted themselves to the apostles' teaching and the fellowship, to the breaking of bread and the prayers. And awe came upon every soul, and many wonders and signs were being done through the apostles. And all who believed were together and had all things in common. And they were selling their possessions and belongings and distributing the proceeds to all, as any had need. And day by day, attending the temple together and breaking bread in their homes, they received their food with glad and generous hearts, praising God and having favor with all the people. And the Lord added to their number day by day those who were being saved.

Look at what the early church actually did. They devoted themselves to teaching, fellowship, breaking bread, and prayer, sharing what they had and eating together in one another's homes with glad and generous hearts. And the Lord added to their number day by day, not because they ran a great program, but because a family was simply living out the gospel in real life.
5.1 - When you read about the early church in Acts 2, what stands out to you most? What part of their life sounds most appealing to you right now?
You Already Belong

Here is the most important thing to understand about the church: you do not earn your seat at the table, you were given one. When God saved you, He adopted you as His child, and because every believer shares the same Father, that makes everyone in the church your brother or sister. You are not a guest. You are family.

This matters because many people walk into a church and wonder whether they really belong. They feel too new, too broken, too far behind. But belonging in the church does not come from having your life figured out, it comes from being adopted by God. If you are in Christ, you belong. Period. The same grace that saved you is the grace that makes you part of the family.
See what kind of love the Father has given to us, that we should be called children of God; and so we are.
5.2 - Imagine being fully welcomed and finding belonging in a spiritual family at church. What would that look like for you?
The Aspen Grove

blocks.alt: An aspen grove with golden leaves and white bark

blocks.text: Most people think of trees as independent, one tree standing alone on a hillside, surviving on its own roots. But an Aspen grove works completely differently. It looks like a forest of separate trees, but underground the entire grove shares a single root system. They share water, nutrients, and strength. When one part is weak, the rest feeds it. When a storm hits, they hold each other up. And the whole grove grew from one original seedling.

That is a picture of the church. Jesus is the original seed, and because every believer is rooted in Him, we all share the same spiritual root system. You are not a lonely tree stranded on an island hoping to survive the next storm. You are part of a grove, connected underneath it all, even when you cannot see it. The health of the whole grove depends on every tree staying planted and staying connected.
5.3 - What encourages you about the Aspen Grove picture and being connected to other believers as a church?
The Potluck, Not the Restaurant

Many people walk into church with a mindset they do not even realize they have: the restaurant mindset. You show up, professionals serve you, and if the experience was good, maybe you come back next week. But the church was never meant to be a restaurant. It is a potluck. At a potluck, everyone brings something to share. Everyone contributes, and nobody sits in the corner waiting to be served.

When everyone brings what they have, the whole family is fed. When only a few contribute and everyone else consumes, the table gets thin and the few get exhausted. God designed the church so that every member has something to offer. Your gifts, your time, your prayers, your encouragement, your honesty, all of it is needed at the table.
Now you are the body of Christ and individually members of it.

blocks.prompt: If the church is a potluck, what dish are you bringing? Tap the areas where you could see yourself serving, helping, or leading.

blocks.text: Where Are You Planted?

The church is not just an idea to appreciate, it is a family to join. And joining means being known - showing up, letting people into the real and unpolished version of your life, being willing to serve before you feel ready, and being willing to receive help before you feel comfortable.

And let us consider how to stir one another up to love and good works, not neglecting to meet together, as is the habit of some, but encouraging one another, and all the more as you see the Day drawing near.

God did not design you to follow Jesus alone. Life is hard, and it is so much harder alone. The church is the place where you are loved when you are unlovable, carried when you cannot walk, and challenged when you are drifting. And as you grow, you will be the one doing the loving, carrying, and challenging for someone else. That is the potluck. That is the grove. That is the family of God.
5.4 - What is your church family like right now? If you are not involved in a church, what is one step you can take this week? (Ask a Christian where to go, visit some websites for research...)
Lesson 5 Review

You have learned that the church is not a building or an event. It is a family rooted in the gospel, connected like an Aspen grove sharing the same root system, and sustained by every member bringing something to the table. You belong because God adopted you, not because you earned your way in.

blocks.heading: Introduction to John 13, 14, and 15
blocks.intro: In John 13-15, Jesus is with His closest friends on the night before the cross. He washes their feet, gives them a new commandment to love one another the way He has loved them, and promises that the Holy Spirit will come to help them. As you read, notice how Jesus talks about life with God and life with each other as deeply connected. This is a picture of the kind of community He is calling the church to be.

blocks.text: Closing Reflections
What is one key thing you learned from this lesson, and what next step can you take this week?
What have you learned about God and yourself recently in your Daily Devotions?
What is a prayer request for this week?
